speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 4 of the interview with Moshe Taube, March 7th, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: Life in ghetto was really [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] full of anguish, full of forebodings, sufferings, and deprivation. But we did not feel yet that our lives, our very lives, were threatened. We knew that people were taken off the streets many times by German parties coming in and-- and-- and catching people in the street for work, mostly for work, and returning them later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did see it, and I was terrified. I was running away, because everybody was running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would you go to hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: In any house, in any cellar, you see, to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long would you have to stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: An hour, two hours, until they left.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that? Do you remember any time like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was during the day, mostly in the morning-- morning hours, because they needed somebody to work for them at their place, at the-- at their whatever-- wherever they were stationed. So they just came into the ghetto and grabbed people. But usually they returned them from work in the-- in the evening hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard, if you'd be hiding, for somebody not to make noise sometimes or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This I-- I don't remember really. But mostly the hiding was in order not to be taken from the street somewhere to work under terrible conditions and being beaten while working. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had sufficient-- sufficient food, perhaps, in ghetto-- sufficient bread, I would say. Not always. Sometimes, as I said, I would-- I was hungry. Sometimes I was hungry because it was not a regular food supply. Sometimes there was-- there was-- there were days that food did not reach the-- the ghetto. The usual sources of food were not there, perhaps, so people were suffering hunger. But this was, I would say, an exception to the rule. The rule was there was enough-- enough bread, enough potatoes, and some vegetables, I would say. There was not-- no variety in food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still involved in music at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: My music involvement at that time was singing during services. And I was-- as a matter of fact, my mentor, Cantor Mandelbaum, [NON-ENGLISH], was also in Ghetto Cracow. And he was also officiating or singing for-- at the services when-- at the-- in the same place where I was attending, the same room.

speaker: interviewer
text: He used to come there often and sing for us and officiate and lead the services. So this was my only exposure in the ghetto to music-- singing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Social life in ghetto? None whatsoever. Fun? No, of course not. Were people getting married at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very few, very few, very few. It was almost-- it was an event of-- of-- of-- of exception, I would say, to get married in the ghetto. It was very rare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go to a marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in the ghetto. I heard of somebody getting married, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, were children being born?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I do not-- I do not remember attending a bris or-- or anything in the ghetto. No, no, no, no, no, no, I don't remember that. Because who would want to bring a child to a-- to a world that was so fraught with uncertainty, where there was no future? Whoever would want this? Although we, as I said before, we-- our lives, we did not feel that our very lives were yet threatened, but we did not know how we would get out of all this misery in the ghetto and-- while the Germans had such a-- still such tremendous power over us and-- and in-- in general in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see anyone shot in the ghetto-- get shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This I saw only in the-- in the-- in the Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll talk about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Plaszow camp, this I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the streets in the ghetto like?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were really two streets-- two main streets in the ghetto. People came out sometimes when the-- when there were no Germans. First of all, whenever we came out from-- from-- from this house, we looked whether there are any Germans in the street. If there were no Germans, people came out to greet-- greet each other, to exchange some impressions with one another, to exchange our information-- family information-- with one another, to voice hopes, perhaps, with other, and to [SIGHS] compare notes, compare notes. But when the Germans were walking, there was-- the street was almost empty, because this spelled trouble when they were walking in the streets of ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of children in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were children in the ghetto, yes. There were a lot of children in ghetto. And some of them didn't know what was going on, so they were frolicking, the children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But soon enough, in 19-- the end of 1941-- I think it was November-- the-- the transports started. There were-- the ghetto was surrounded by German-- by the-- by Germans in helmets, in steel helmets with machine guns. And they went with a list of people from-- from house to house, taking people out and towards the transport, towards the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] railway station, where there were loaded in boxcars, in cattle cars, and transported out of Cracow. Nobody knew where. Nobody knew the destination of those trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was the train station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Within walking distance, about 2 miles, I would say, about 2 miles. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] By then, we didn't-- but you see, the Germans were so ingenious to try to cover their footprints, their murderous footprints, that they made somebody, before they are put to death, write a postcard-- we are OK, and we are going to work-- and send it to their dear ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one, two, or three postcards like this came back, and immediately there was hope, and-- and everybody was talking. There was a postcard here and a postcard there, to this and that family, and the people who were transported out are OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was a German scheme, of course, to keep the lid-- to keep the lid over the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of the Jews, that they should still maintain some hope of-- for those who were transported away. But of course, these were-- they were all put to death without an exception. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This started in 1941 and continued 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the mood?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mood became blacker and blacker all the time, because, after all, peoples did not know-- did not know what-- what's-- what was happening. They did not know about the Wannsee Conference in German, that-- that the Final Solution was-- was decided upon-- the Final Solution. They didn't know about it. Nobody knew about it. This was a tightly kept secret by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people you knew taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all the time, my-- my family, members of my family, my-- on my mother's side, of course. They're all the time were taking away, for-- with every transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know in advance or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody knew in advance. There were-- there were rumors that tomorrow or-- or the day after or in-- in three days, there is going to be a-- a transport again. There were rumors, unconfirmed. But those rumors were always right. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When we speak about 1942, October 1942, my mother and my sister-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as a matter-- we all went with the transports. This was-- this was one of the biggest round-ups of the people of the ghetto, in October 20-- October 22, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the ghetto was surrounded by-- by the German military, by SS with machine guns, with-- with posts, with, you know, like-- like regular siege, a wartime siege of the ghetto, so nobody should-- and they rounded up everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come to your building or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody. Yes, of course. There-- there was a whole army, detachments of German soldiers coming, SS coming and-- and-- and rounding up people and pushing them towards the plaza, to the-- to-- towards the square, the big square, where-- where everybody was-- was gathered there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could people hide or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Whoever hid was shot. I heard many shots. People who were hiding were taken out and shot right on the spot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So when my-- yeah, so we went all with the transport. We were already on the way to the rail-- the railroad station, or railroad gathering place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my father and my mother and my sister. All of a sudden, I saw a German on the side gathering a small group of the people where I was working for the Germans. You know, there was an outpost that I was working, the-- the-- the-- it was called Sonderdienst, S-O-N-D-E-R-D-I-N-S-T, Sonderdienst. In 1942, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was detached with my father to work for that particular German outpost, or place of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think destiny. It was destiny. We were just commanded to go there, and we worked there day by day, every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get a chance to say good-bye to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because while we were walking, I saw the German, and I felt like an angel pushed me towards that German. Because the-- the German was the-- the overseer of our work detachment in Sonderdienst.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thiele. His name was Thiele, T-H-I-E-L-E. This was his name. He was standing there and taking out his people in the group to stand with him. And I felt-- I saw him, I felt like I was propelled by a divine force, by an angel, towards him. I was going together with my mother. All of a sudden, I felt propelled towards him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I stood there, and he said to me, OK, you want to work? Stay here. And I saw that my mother pushed my father out from the group going to the-- to the railway station. She pushed my father, and she told him, go join him. Join-- join Moshe. Join [NON-ENGLISH]. Did you see her face?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was crying, and my-- my sister was crying. It was a-- one of the most tragic moments of my life. But this was the moment that my life was saved, that I live. By divine intervention, I was saved. Because I could have gone to-- and not-- not noticed the German. Or even if I noticed the German, I-- I would want to stay with all the group, with my mother and sister and go together with them to Belzec. But I was propelled, as I said before. I was propelled. I was pushed out from the group. And my-- my-- my mother asked my father-- or she pushed him. She said, go to-- and he went together with me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she went with the transport, and I remained with the work detachment, with Thiele. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then came the SS overseer of the-- of the action. He says, who are those Jews? And Thiele stood at attention. Herr Obersturmbannfuhrer-- his name was Haase, H-A-S-- H-A-A-S-E. This was the major of-- of the SS who-- who [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oversaw this action. So he stood at-- Thiele stood at attention. This is the group that I am requesting that should be apportioned to our workplace. So he looked at-- looked at us, this SS major, he said, but if you don't work, you are-- you are going to hear from me if you don't work diligently. And he left us. What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very handsome, very handsome-looking man, with-- with epaulets and-- you know how those German SS people looked. Haase, he was-- he was extremely dignified-looking.

speaker: interviewer
text: You understood what he said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I understood. At that time, I already understood some German because I-- I was exposed to German for years now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is how I was saved on October 22, 1942. And my mother and sister went to Belzec to be killed because they were Jews-- because they were Jews. This was the only reason. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remained in the ghetto [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with my father and with some-- with the remainder of the Jews who somehow saved themselves also through-- through work, or some German took them under their so-called fatherly wings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The minority of Jews remained, because the majority was really transported. The ghetto was shrunk. It was cut in half and shrunk at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what type of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, in Sonderdienst, all the time in Sonderdienst.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear of Schindler at this time at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Afterwards, about one month or two months after the-- after-- in October-- after October 22-- I think it was either November or December-- my father and-- my father got some pull, you know, in-- in the ghetto, and he was engaged by Madritsch. Madritsch figures very prominently in the-- in the Keneally book about-- Schindler's Keneally book. This was a-- a factory that manufactured the uniforms for Germans, all kinds of uniforms. So my father was engaged by Madritsch, and he took me along. So I worked as a buttonhole maker, a machine buttonhole maker, for the-- for that firm starting November 1942. From ghetto, we went to-- to-- to the factory in group, and we were returned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, yes. This was every day, seven days a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not far. It was within walking distance. It was about half an hour walk each-- each way. Come 1943--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was overseeing some-- some-- was like overseer of-- he checked the-- the-- the uniforms after they were finished to-- to-- to-- to-- to see whether everything is in order, where-- where the-- there are no-- no mistakes or any defects or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you at the job?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Being in the ghetto, it was-- it was the ghetto life. It was ghetto life-- going to work, come late, and collapsing, [SIGHS] nothing more. Only there was ceaseless sobbing and crying after losing a mother and sister. I still didn't know that I lost them, but I had a feeling. I had an inner feeling that I won't see them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father say?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was crying with me, together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was crying with me, because we had a-- we had a premonition, a very bad premonition. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] March 19-- 13, 1943, we are rounded up. The entire ghetto was rounded up and transported to Plaszow camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the round-up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody was ordered to stand in the street, and the Germans came and checked, because everybody had a-- some kind of a identification. They checked. They counted everyone. Every group had a-- every group had to belong to a working place, you see? And thus, from the ghetto, we were marched to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there any bystanders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. The Pole-- the-- the Poles were-- were looking at us, staring, and enjoying the sight of Jews being-- being marched, you know, like-- like prisoners of war, or worse, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the camp at Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they saying anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were just staring and enjoying the sight. That's all. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In Plaszow, it was already a different story. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You-- you remember from the movie, there was Amon Goeth, the commandant of the Plaszow. He was a killer, a real killer, a sadistic killer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He killed for pleasure, for his own personal pleasure. And at that time, I saw people being killed in the-- in the-- in-- in the camp for not saying that what he was doing is right and accepted by them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he used to go and he asked a group of people, who doesn't like here what I-- what I am doing in this camp, shooting people? Who doesn't like it? So nobody answered. Ah, I see you don't like it. Out-- shot him. Ah, you don't seem to like my actions, my deportment, my behavior. Come out-- boom. So the people saw what's going on, so started crying. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jews, have-- have mercy. Say something here. So everybody starts, oh, we are very happy what you are doing. You are-- you are a-- you are a great man. We are-- you're very-- you are doing the right thing. This put him to-- to rest, in other words, that this-- this stilled his rabid feelings. What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Extremely husky man, tall, you know, and with the German boots, with a-- with a whip in his hand, and a gun, of course. The gun was mostly in his hands. But usually, he has-- he had both the gun and-- and the-- and the whip in his-- in his hands, in one hand and the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a-- a dog?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had three dogs. And he kept the dogs just to-- to be able to-- to wound or kill for his own pleasure. One dog was named Rolf, R-O-L-F. He was a-- a killer like himself. He used to incite the-- the dogs. And if-- if he didn't like somebody, instead of killing him by a-- by a gun, he wanted to see him torn by the dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I saw it once, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Faintly, faintly. I remember it faintly. I know that the dog-- the dog was-- had-- had a-- big, a big dog with-- with white with-- he looked like a dalmatian, but really, it was more than a dalmatian. It was some-- some-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he had black spots, as I remember, the dog, one of them. He had three, of course. One was a German shepherd. I don't know his name. But of course, the-- Amon Goeth's story and history is well-- very well documented in the film and also in Keneally's book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him shoot people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. Goeth, Amon Goeth? Sure, sure. He shot our-- you see, every barracks-- we were in-- in barracks in the camp. Every barrack had a-- a-- a-- a-- somebody who-- who was responsible for-- for-- for cleanliness, for-- for discipline, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Goeth came in once one-- one morning, and he found something irregular, something irregular. He says, who is-- who is responsible? Who is-- who-- who's here, the barrack, responsible, the-- the-- the barrack foreman? So I'm [? one. ?] What is this here? There's some-- something irregular, some-- some dirt, some-- come out, and he shot him. He told him, knee, kneel down. And the man was, of course-- I have-- he said, I have a wife, and I have children. Please. Kneel down, and he shot him. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot. I forgot his name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes. It was a-- it was a tremendous traumatic experience, traumatic experience. I still can see this man in my-- in my nightmares, in a pool-- lying in a pool of blood, like in my nightmares. And I have very often nightmares at night pertaining to my history and pertaining to-- to the Holocaust and to the years-- my-- my years of 1939 and-- and '40-- to '45. [SIGHS]
